title: 2.8 Transport in Plants
description: Transport in plants — every fact from the textbook.

questions.question: What are the transporting vessels of higher plants collectively called?
questions.correctAnswer: Vascular bundles

questions.question: What are the two types of transporting vessels in plants?
questions.correctAnswer: Xylem and phloem

questions.question: What does xylem transport?
options: Water and minerals from root to leaf
questions.correctAnswer: Water and minerals from root to leaf

questions.question: What type of cells make up xylem?
options: Living cells arranged end to end
Elongated dead cells joined end to end to form continuous vessels
questions.correctAnswer: Elongated dead cells joined end to end to form continuous vessels

questions.question: What does phloem transport?
options: Organic matter (food) manufactured in the leaf to the rest of the plant
questions.correctAnswer: Organic matter (food) manufactured in the leaf to the rest of the plant

questions.question: What type of cells make up phloem?
options: Dead cells arranged end to end
Living cells arranged end to end
questions.correctAnswer: Living cells arranged end to end

questions.question: What is the transport of food through phloem called?
questions.correctAnswer: Translocation

questions.question: What substances does phloem transport?
questions.correctAnswer: Sucrose and amino acids

questions.question: Can phloem transport food up and down the plant?
questions.correctAnswer: Yes, both up and down

questions.question: What are sites of food production (sources) and sites of use or storage (sinks) connected by?
questions.correctAnswer: Translocation through phloem

questions.question: Why is the stem of sugar cane so sweet?
options: Sugars synthesized in the leaf are converted to starch and accumulated in storage organs
questions.correctAnswer: Sugars synthesized in the leaf are converted to starch and accumulated in storage organs

questions.question: What are examples of storage organs in plants?
options: Root and stem tubers, leaves, seeds and fruits
questions.correctAnswer: Root and stem tubers, leaves, seeds and fruits

questions.question: How does water from the soil first enter the root?
options: Through root hairs, which are elongated single cells
questions.correctAnswer: Through root hairs, which are elongated single cells

questions.question: What do root hairs provide that allows more water to enter the root?
questions.correctAnswer: A large surface area

questions.question: What is the process of water and minerals entering the root called?
questions.correctAnswer: Absorption

questions.question: How does water pass from cell to cell across the root?
options: Either by osmosis across the cell membrane and cytoplasm or by diffusion along the porous cell wall
questions.correctAnswer: Either by osmosis across the cell membrane and cytoplasm or by diffusion along the porous cell wall

questions.question: Does water pass actively or passively across the root cells?
questions.correctAnswer: Passively (without spending additional energy)

questions.question: What is the main force responsible for pulling water from root to leaves through xylem?
questions.correctAnswer: Transpiration pull

questions.question: What creates the transpiration pull?
questions.correctAnswer: Water evaporating from leaves

questions.question: What helps resist the downward pull of gravity on water in the xylem?
options: Adhesion of water to the cell wall by hydrogen bonds
questions.correctAnswer: Adhesion of water to the cell wall by hydrogen bonds

questions.question: What helps hold together the column of water within xylem cells?
options: Cohesion due to hydrogen bonds between water molecules
questions.correctAnswer: Cohesion due to hydrogen bonds between water molecules

questions.question: In what form do minerals enter the root?
questions.correctAnswer: Ionic (charged) form

questions.question: How are minerals taken up when concentration is higher in plant cells than outside?
options: Actively by cell spending energy against a concentration gradient
questions.correctAnswer: Actively by cell spending energy against a concentration gradient

questions.question: What is the ringing or girdling experiment used to demonstrate?
questions.correctAnswer: Food transport through phloem

questions.question: What happens in the ringing experiment?
options: A ring of bark with the phloem is removed, leaving xylem as the only vessel connecting upper and lower parts
questions.correctAnswer: A ring of bark with the phloem is removed, leaving xylem as the only vessel connecting upper and lower parts

questions.question: What is observed in the upper part of the girdled area after a few days?
options: A swelling due to accumulation of food materials translocated from the leaf
questions.correctAnswer: A swelling due to accumulation of food materials translocated from the leaf

questions.question: What are aphids?
options: Soft-bodied insects that use piercing and sucking mouth parts to feed on phloem sap
questions.correctAnswer: Soft-bodied insects that use piercing and sucking mouth parts to feed on phloem sap

questions.question: What will ultimately happen to a plant that is ringed?
options: It will die because of disruption of food transport through the phloem
It will develop a new phloem layer
questions.correctAnswer: It will die because of disruption of food transport through the phloem

questions.question: Which part of a ringed plant dies first?
questions.correctAnswer: The root

questions.question: Why does the root of a ringed plant die first?
options: Because water supply is cut off
Because supply of food to the root is discontinued
Because oxygen supply is cut off
questions.correctAnswer: Because supply of food to the root is discontinued

questions.question: What type of transport occurs through the phloem?
options: Active transport where living phloem cells use energy obtained by metabolic process
questions.correctAnswer: Active transport where living phloem cells use energy obtained by metabolic process
